# Problem statement

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

```latex
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}
\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}

\newcommand{\OP}{\operatorname{OP}}
\newcommand{\inv}{\operatorname{inv}}
\newcommand{\Hilb}{\operatorname{Hilb}}
\newcommand{\Stir}{\operatorname{Stir}}

\begin{document}

\begin{center}
{\bf Hook coefficients in the universal Hilbert series of diagonal coinvariants}
\end{center}

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

I interpret \(R_n^{(m)}\) as the diagonal coinvariant algebra
\[
 R_n^{(m)}=\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m]\,
/\,\langle \mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\rangle,
\]
where \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts by simultaneously permuting the subscript \(i\) in all
\(m\) sets of variables.  Thus the coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the stable
multiplicities of the polynomial \(GL_m\)-irreducibles \(S_\lambda(\mathbb C^m)\).

\section*{The combinatorial model}

For \(r,k\ge 1\), let \(\OP_{r,k}\) be the set of ordered set partitions
\(\sigma=(B_1|\cdots|B_k)\) of \([r]\) into \(k\) nonempty blocks.  An inversion of
\(\sigma\) is a pair \((i,j)\) such that
\[
        i<j,\qquad i=\min B_m,\qquad j\in B_\ell,\qquad \ell<m .
\]
Let \(\inv(\sigma)\) be the number of such pairs.  When \(k=r\), this is the
usual inversion number of the permutation obtained by reading the singleton
blocks from left to right.

\begin{theorem}
Let \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) be a hook, with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\).  Then
\(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is the number of ordered set partitions
\(\sigma=(B_1|\cdots|B_k)\) of \([r]\), for some \(r\le n\), such that
\[
        r-k=b+1,\qquad r\hbox{ is not a singleton block of }\sigma,\qquad
        \inv(\sigma)=a-1 .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\,q^{a-1}z^b
=
\sum_{\substack{2\le r\le n\\1\le k\le r-1}}
\ \sum_{\substack{\sigma\in \OP_{r,k}\\
                  r\text{ not singleton in }\sigma}}
        q^{\inv(\sigma)}z^{r-k-1}.
\]
The empty partition coefficient is \(c_{\varnothing}(n)=1\).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
We use two standard inputs.  First, diagonal supersymmetry says that the same
universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur if one replaces all but one
bosonic set of variables by one fermionic set.  Thus the Hilbert series of the
superspace coinvariant ring
\[
 SR_n=\bigl(\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes
          \bigwedge(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)\bigr)
/\langle(\mathbb C[x]\otimes\bigwedge\theta)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\rangle
\]
has the expansion
\[
        \Hilb(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/z),
\]
where \(q\) records \(x\)-degree and \(z\) records \(\theta\)-degree.  In one
bosonic and one fermionic variable, \(s_\lambda(q/z)\) vanishes unless
\(\lambda\) is a hook, and
\[
        s_{(a,1^b)}(q/z)=q^a z^b+q^{a-1}z^{b+1}
        =q^{a-1}z^b(q+z).
\]
Therefore
\[
        \Hilb(SR_n;q,z)
        =1+(q+z)\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
              c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b .          \tag{1}
\]

Second, the Hilbert series of \(SR_n\) is
\[
        H_n(q,z):=\Hilb(SR_n;q,z)
        =\sum_{k=1}^{n} z^{\,n-k}[k]!_q\,\Stir_q(n,k),                 \tag{2}
\]
where \([k]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{k-1}\), \([k]!_q=[k]_q[k-1]_q\cdots[1]_q\), and
\[
        \Stir_q(n,k)=\Stir_q(n-1,k-1)+[k]_q\Stir_q(n-1,k).
\]
Put \(F_{r,k}=[k]!_q\Stir_q(r,k)\).  From the recurrence,
\[
        F_{r,k}=[k]_qF_{r-1,k-1}+[k]_qF_{r-1,k}.
\]
Using \([k+1]_q=1+q[k]_q\) in (2), we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
H_n(q,z)
&=\sum_{k=1}^{n}z^{n-k}\bigl([k]_qF_{n-1,k-1}
                         +[k]_qF_{n-1,k}\bigr)\\
&=H_{n-1}(q,z)
 +(q+z)\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}[k]_qF_{n-1,k}z^{n-1-k}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Since \(H_1(q,z)=1\), iteration gives
\[
        \frac{H_n(q,z)-1}{q+z}
        =
        \sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\sum_{k=1}^{r}
             [k]_q\,F_{r,k}\,z^{r-k}.                    \tag{3}
\]

It remains to identify the right side of (3).  The polynomial \(F_{r,k}\) is
the inversion enumerator of ordered set partitions:
\[
        F_{r,k}=\sum_{\tau\in \OP_{r,k}}q^{\inv(\tau)}.                \tag{4}
\]
Indeed, both sides satisfy the same recurrence.  To see this directly, insert
the largest letter \(r\) into an ordered partition of \([r-1]\).  If \(r\) is
inserted as a singleton block, or into an existing block, at position \(i\) from
the left, the new inversions involving \(r\) are exactly the minima of the
blocks to its right, so their number is \(k-i\).  Summing over \(i=1,\ldots,k\)
contributes \([k]_q\), giving the recurrence above and the initial condition.

Now restrict this insertion argument to insertions of \(r+1\) into an existing
block of an ordered partition \(\tau\in\OP_{r,k}\).  The result is precisely an
ordered partition \(\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\) in which \(r+1\) is not a singleton,
and the extra inversion contribution is again one of \(0,1,\ldots,k-1\).
Hence
\[
 [k]_qF_{r,k}
 =
 \sum_{\substack{\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\\ r+1\text{ not singleton}}}
      q^{\inv(\sigma)} .
\]
Substituting this into (3), and comparing with (1), gives exactly
\[
\sum_{a,b}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b
=
\sum_{\substack{2\le r\le n\\1\le k\le r-1}}
\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in \OP_{r,k}\\r\text{ not singleton}}}
q^{\inv(\sigma)}z^{r-k-1}.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^{a-1}z^b\) proves the claimed interpretation.
\end{proof}

Adv. Math. 239 (2013), 97--108.

\bibitem{Lentfer}

\bibitem{RhoadesWilson}
B. Rhoades and A. T. Wilson,
\emph{The Hilbert series of the superspace coinvariant ring},
Forum Math. Pi 12 (2024), e16.

\end{thebibliography}

\end{document}

```

```latex
\[
R_n^{(m)}=\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m]/

\]

(Lentfer 2025), the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\) has Hilbert series
\[
H_n(q,z)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/z).
\]
For one bosonic variable \(q\) and one fermionic variable \(z\), only hooks survive:
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/z)=q^az^b+q^{a-1}z^{b+1}=q^{a-1}z^b(q+z),
\]
so
\[
H_n(q,z)=1+(q+z)\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b.
\]

Rhoades--Wilson prove
\[
H_n(q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n z^{n-k}[k]!_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
where \(\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)=\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k-1)+[k]_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(n-1,k)\).

\[
F_{r,k}=[k]_qF_{r-1,k-1}+[k]_qF_{r-1,k}.
\]
This implies
\[
H_n=H_{n-1}+(q+z)\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}[k]_qF_{n-1,k}z^{n-1-k},
\]
since \([k+1]_q=1+q[k]_q\).  Therefore
\[
\frac{H_n-1}{q+z}=\sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\sum_{k=1}^r [k]_qF_{r,k}z^{r-k}.
\]

Combinatorial interpretation: \(F_{r,k}=\sum_{\sigma\in OP_{r,k}}q^{\operatorname{inv}(\sigma)}\), where an inversion of
\(\sigma=(B_1|\cdots|B_k)\) is a pair \(i<j\) with \(i=\min B_m\), \(j\in B_\ell\), and \(\ell<m\).
Insertion of the largest letter into position \(i\) creates \(k-i\) inversions.
Thus \([k]F_{r,k}\) counts ordered set partitions of \([r+1]\) into \(k\) blocks in which \(r+1\) is not a singleton, by inversion number.
Consequently \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts ordered set partitions of \([r]\), \(r\le n\), with \(r-k=b+1\), largest \(r\) not singleton, and \(\operatorname{inv}=a-1\).

\[
C_0=(1+q)(1+q+q^2)(1+q+q^2+q^3),\quad
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,\quad C_2=q.

C_1=6q+16q^2+26q^3+29q^4+24q^5+14q^6+5q^7+q^8,

C_2=4q+9q^2+10q^3+5q^4+q^5,\quad C_3=q.
\]
```

## Referee report

### Overall verdict

I find the submitted `answer.tex` mathematically valid and essentially complete. The proof correctly reduces hook Schur coefficients to the one-boson/one-fermion superspace coinvariant Hilbert series, uses the Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert series formula, and gives a correct ordered-set-partition interpretation. I found only minor expository issues, not fatal gaps.

### LaTeX contract audit

- `\documentclass[12pt]{article}` is used exactly.
- `fullpage` is used; no forbidden margin/layout packages or manual layout changes appear.
- No forbidden line-spacing or in-document font-size commands appear.
- I recreated and compiled `answer.tex` with `pdflatex`; compilation succeeded.
- The resulting PDF is 3 pages, within the 12-page limit.
- `references.bib` is unused, but `answer.tex` contains its own bibliography and is standalone.

### Literature validation

The interpretation of \(R_n^{(m)}\) as the diagonal coinvariant algebra is consistent with Bergeron’s definition of diagonal coinvariant spaces as quotients by the ideal generated by constant-term-free diagonal invariants. Bergeron also explains that the multigraded Hilbert series is the \(GL_r\)-character and expands in Schur polynomials with multiplicity coefficients independent of the number \(r\) of variable sets. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1105.4358))

The use of diagonal supersymmetry is supported by Lentfer’s theorem: bosonic–fermionic coinvariant rings have universal super-Schur coefficients independent of the number of bosonic and fermionic alphabets, and the symmetric-group corollary gives the corresponding Frobenius expansion. Forgetting the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-module structure gives the Hilbert-series coefficients used in the answer. Lentfer also defines the super Schur functions \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) in the needed positive form. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The formula
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n z^{n-k}[k]!_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)
\]
and the recurrence for \(\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)\) are exactly the Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert series formula for the superspace coinvariant ring. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/services/aop-cambridge-core/content/view/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8/S2050508624000143a.pdf/div-class-title-the-hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring-div.pdf))

### Paragraph-by-paragraph mathematical audit

1. **Problem statement and interpretation.**  
   The interpretation is explicit and faithful to the standard diagonal coinvariant algebra. The statement that \(c_\lambda(n)\) are stable \(GL_m\)-multiplicities is correct: the multigraded Hilbert series is the diagonal-torus character of the \(GL_m\)-module.

2. **Definition of ordered set partitions and inversions.**  
   The inversion statistic is well-defined. For singleton blocks it agrees with the ordinary inversion number of the corresponding permutation. No issue.

3. **The theorem statement.**  
   The count over ordered set partitions of \([r]\) for \(2\le r\le n\), with \(r\) not singleton and \(r-k=b+1\), is finite and unambiguous. The edge case \(n=1\) is handled correctly: the sum is empty and only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\) remains.

4. **Use of diagonal supersymmetry.**  
   This is the main external input. It is valid, but the answer would be clearer if it explicitly said that Lentfer’s universal coefficients for the Frobenius/character series become the Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) after taking dimensions of the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-irreducible factors. This is not a mathematical gap.

5. **Specialization of super Schur functions.**  
   The claim
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/z)=q^az^b+q^{a-1}z^{b+1}
   \]
   is correct for one bosonic and one fermionic variable. Non-hooks vanish because the condition for nonzero \(s_\lambda(q/z)\) is \(\lambda_2\le 1\). The notation \(q/z\) could be briefly defined as super-Schur notation, but the formula itself is enough for the proof.

6. **Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert series and algebraic recurrence.**  
   The recurrence manipulation from
   \[
   H_n(q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n z^{n-k}F_{n,k}
   \]
   to
   \[
   \frac{H_n(q,z)-1}{q+z}
   =
   \sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\sum_{k=1}^r [k]_qF_{r,k}z^{r-k}
   \]
   is correct. Minor improvement: state the standard boundary conditions
   \(\operatorname{Stir}_q(0,0)=1\), \(\operatorname{Stir}_q(r,0)=0\) for \(r>0\), or equivalently \(F_{r,k}=0\) outside \(1\le k\le r\).

7. **Identification of \(F_{r,k}\) with the inversion enumerator.**  
   The insertion proof is correct. Inserting the largest letter into position \(i\) creates exactly \(k-i\) new inversions, giving the factor \([k]_q\). The two cases—largest letter inserted as a singleton or into an existing block—match the two recurrence terms.

8. **Restricted insertion and final coefficient extraction.**  
   Restricting to insertion of \(r+1\) into an existing block correctly gives ordered set partitions of \([r+1]\) with \(r+1\) not singleton. The reindexing \(r+1\mapsto r\) gives the exponent \(z^{r-k-1}\), so the conditions \(r-k=b+1\) and \(\operatorname{inv}=a-1\) are exactly right.

9. **Computational cross-check.**  
   I enumerated ordered set partitions and compared the claimed generating function with \((H_n(q,z)-1)/(q+z)\) computed from the Rhoades–Wilson formula for \(1\le n\le 6\). No discrepancies occurred. For example, for \(n=4\) the ordered-partition side gives
   \[
   3+5q+6q^2+5q^3+3q^4+q^5
   +(3+5q+4q^2+q^3)z+z^2,
   \]
   matching the Hilbert-series extraction.

10. **Working notes.**  
   No fatal issue appears in the scratchpad. One sanity-check line for \(C_0\) appears to mix in the empty coefficient or use a shifted convention, but the actual `answer.tex` theorem and proof use the correct \(q^{a-1}\) normalization.

### Required corrections?

None required for mathematical validity. Recommended but nonessential edits: add the \(q\)-Stirling initial conditions, define \(s_\lambda(q/z)\) as a super Schur function, and spell out the passage from Lentfer’s character coefficients to Hilbert coefficients.

```latex
\(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is the number of ordered set partitions
\(\sigma=(B_1|\cdots|B_k)\) of \([r]\), for some \(2\le r\le n\), such that
\[
        r-k=b+1,\qquad r\hbox{ is not a singleton block of }\sigma,\qquad
        \inv(\sigma)=a-1 .

\[
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\,q^{a-1}z^b

\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/z)\) denotes the super Schur polynomial in one even variable
\(q\) and one odd variable \(z\).  With one even and one odd variable,
\(s_\lambda(q/z)\) vanishes unless \(\lambda\) lies in the \((1,1)\)-hook, i.e.
unless \(\lambda\) is a hook, and
\[
        s_{(a,1^b)}(q/z)=q^a z^b+q^{a-1}z^{b+1}
        =q^{a-1}z^b(q+z).
\]
Thus
\[
        \Hilb(SR_n;q,z)
        =1+(q+z)\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
              c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b .          \tag{1}
\]

Second, the Hilbert series of \(SR_n\), due to Rhoades--Wilson, is
\[
        H_n(q,z):=\Hilb(SR_n;q,z)
        =\sum_{k=1}^{n} z^{\,n-k}[k]!_q\,\Stir_q(n,k),                 \tag{2}
\]
where \([k]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{k-1}\), \([k]!_q=[k]_q[k-1]_q\cdots[1]_q\), and
\[
        \Stir_q(r,k)=\Stir_q(r-1,k-1)+[k]_q\Stir_q(r-1,k).
\]
The boundary conditions are
\(\Stir_q(0,0)=1\), \(\Stir_q(r,0)=0\) for \(r>0\), and
\(\Stir_q(r,k)=0\) outside \(0\le k\le r\).  Put
\(F_{r,k}=[k]!_q\Stir_q(r,k)\).  Then \(F_{r,k}=0\) outside
\(1\le k\le r\), except for the harmless convention \(F_{0,0}=1\), and
\[
        F_{r,k}=[k]_qF_{r-1,k-1}+[k]_qF_{r-1,k}.
\]
Using \([k+1]_q=1+q[k]_q\) in (2), we get, for \(n\ge2\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
H_n(q,z)
&=\sum_{k=1}^{n}z^{n-k}\bigl([k]_qF_{n-1,k-1}
                         +[k]_qF_{n-1,k}\bigr)\\
&=H_{n-1}(q,z)
 +(q+z)\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}[k]_qF_{n-1,k}z^{n-1-k}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Since \(H_1(q,z)=1\), iteration gives
\[
        \frac{H_n(q,z)-1}{q+z}
        =
        \sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\sum_{k=1}^{r}
             [k]_q\,F_{r,k}\,z^{r-k}.                    \tag{3}
\]

It remains to identify the right side of (3).  The polynomial \(F_{r,k}\) is
the inversion enumerator of ordered set partitions:
\[
        F_{r,k}=\sum_{\tau\in \OP_{r,k}}q^{\inv(\tau)}.                \tag{4}
\]
Indeed, both sides satisfy the same recurrence.  To see this directly, insert
the largest letter \(r\) into an ordered partition of \([r-1]\).  To obtain
\(k\) final blocks, either insert \(r\) as a singleton block into one of the
\(k\) possible final block positions, or insert \(r\) into one of the \(k\)
existing blocks.  If the final position is \(i\) from the left, the new
inversions involving \(r\) are exactly the minima of the blocks to its right, so
their number is \(k-i\).  Summing over \(i=1,\ldots,k\) contributes
\([k]_q\) in each of the two cases, giving the recurrence above and the initial
condition.

Now restrict this insertion argument to insertions of \(r+1\) into an existing
block of an ordered partition \(\tau\in\OP_{r,k}\).  The result is precisely an
ordered partition \(\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\) in which \(r+1\) is not a singleton,
and the extra inversion contribution is again one of \(0,1,\ldots,k-1\).
Hence
\[
 [k]_qF_{r,k}
 =
 \sum_{\substack{\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\\ r+1\text{ not singleton}}}
      q^{\inv(\sigma)} .
\]
Substituting this into (3), and replacing \(r+1\) by \(r\), gives exactly
\[
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b
=
\sum_{\substack{2\le r\le n\\1\le k\le r-1}}
\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in \OP_{r,k}\\r\text{ not singleton}}}
q^{\inv(\sigma)}z^{r-k-1}.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^{a-1}z^b\) proves the claimed interpretation.
\end{proof}
```

\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}
\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}

\newcommand{\OP}{\operatorname{OP}}
\newcommand{\inv}{\operatorname{inv}}
\newcommand{\Hilb}{\operatorname{Hilb}}
\newcommand{\Stir}{\operatorname{Stir}}

\begin{document}

\begin{center}
{\bf Hook coefficients in the universal Hilbert series of diagonal coinvariants}
\end{center}

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

I interpret \(R_n^{(m)}\) as the diagonal coinvariant algebra
\[
 R_n^{(m)}=\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m]\,
/\,\langle \mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\rangle,
\]
where \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts by simultaneously permuting the subscript \(i\) in all
\(m\) sets of variables.  Thus the coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the stable
multiplicities of the polynomial \(GL_m\)-irreducibles \(S_\lambda(\mathbb C^m)\).

\section*{The combinatorial model}

For \(r,k\ge 1\), let \(\OP_{r,k}\) be the set of ordered set partitions
\(\sigma=(B_1|\cdots|B_k)\) of \([r]\) into \(k\) nonempty blocks.  An inversion of
\(\sigma\) is a pair \((i,j)\) such that
\[
        i<j,\qquad i=\min B_m,\qquad j\in B_\ell,\qquad \ell<m .
\]
Let \(\inv(\sigma)\) be the number of such pairs.  When \(k=r\), this is the
usual inversion number of the permutation obtained by reading the singleton
blocks from left to right.

\begin{theorem}
Let \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) be a hook, with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\).  Then
\(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is the number of ordered set partitions
\(\sigma=(B_1|\cdots|B_k)\) of \([r]\), for some \(2\le r\le n\), such that
\[
        r-k=b+1,\qquad r\hbox{ is not a singleton block of }\sigma,\qquad
        \inv(\sigma)=a-1 .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\,q^{a-1}z^b
=
\sum_{\substack{2\le r\le n\\1\le k\le r-1}}
\ \sum_{\substack{\sigma\in \OP_{r,k}\\
                  r\text{ not singleton in }\sigma}}
        q^{\inv(\sigma)}z^{r-k-1}.
\]
The empty partition coefficient is \(c_{\varnothing}(n)=1\).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
We use two standard inputs.  First, diagonal supersymmetry says that the
universal Schur coefficients for diagonal coinvariants can be read from the
coinvariant ring with one bosonic alphabet and one fermionic alphabet,
\[
 SR_n=\bigl(\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes
          \bigwedge(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)\bigr)
/\langle(\mathbb C[x]\otimes\bigwedge\theta)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\rangle .
\]
More explicitly, the supersymmetric Frobenius/character expansion has the same
universal multiplicity modules as the purely bosonic expansion; after applying
the dimension map to the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-Frobenius factor one obtains exactly
the Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) appearing in the question.  Therefore
\[
        \Hilb(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/z),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/z)\) denotes the super Schur polynomial in one even variable
\(q\) and one odd variable \(z\).  With one even and one odd variable,
\(s_\lambda(q/z)\) vanishes unless \(\lambda\) lies in the \((1,1)\)-hook, i.e.
unless \(\lambda\) is a hook, and
\[
        s_{(a,1^b)}(q/z)=q^a z^b+q^{a-1}z^{b+1}
        =q^{a-1}z^b(q+z).
\]
Thus
\[
        \Hilb(SR_n;q,z)
        =1+(q+z)\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
              c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b .          \tag{1}
\]

Second, the Hilbert series of \(SR_n\), due to Rhoades--Wilson, is
\[
        H_n(q,z):=\Hilb(SR_n;q,z)
        =\sum_{k=1}^{n} z^{\,n-k}[k]!_q\,\Stir_q(n,k),                 \tag{2}
\]
where \([k]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{k-1}\), \([k]!_q=[k]_q[k-1]_q\cdots[1]_q\), and
\[
        \Stir_q(r,k)=\Stir_q(r-1,k-1)+[k]_q\Stir_q(r-1,k).
\]
The boundary conditions are
\(\Stir_q(0,0)=1\), \(\Stir_q(r,0)=0\) for \(r>0\), and
\(\Stir_q(r,k)=0\) outside \(0\le k\le r\).  Put
\(F_{r,k}=[k]!_q\Stir_q(r,k)\).  Then \(F_{r,k}=0\) outside
\(1\le k\le r\), except for the harmless convention \(F_{0,0}=1\), and
\[
        F_{r,k}=[k]_qF_{r-1,k-1}+[k]_qF_{r-1,k}.
\]
Using \([k+1]_q=1+q[k]_q\) in (2), we get, for \(n\ge2\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
H_n(q,z)
&=\sum_{k=1}^{n}z^{n-k}\bigl([k]_qF_{n-1,k-1}
                         +[k]_qF_{n-1,k}\bigr)\\
&=H_{n-1}(q,z)
 +(q+z)\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}[k]_qF_{n-1,k}z^{n-1-k}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Since \(H_1(q,z)=1\), iteration gives
\[
        \frac{H_n(q,z)-1}{q+z}
        =
        \sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\sum_{k=1}^{r}
             [k]_q\,F_{r,k}\,z^{r-k}.                    \tag{3}
\]

It remains to identify the right side of (3).  The polynomial \(F_{r,k}\) is
the inversion enumerator of ordered set partitions:
\[
        F_{r,k}=\sum_{\tau\in \OP_{r,k}}q^{\inv(\tau)}.                \tag{4}
\]
Indeed, both sides satisfy the same recurrence.  To see this directly, insert
the largest letter \(r\) into an ordered partition of \([r-1]\).  To obtain
\(k\) final blocks, either insert \(r\) as a singleton block into one of the
\(k\) possible final block positions, or insert \(r\) into one of the \(k\)
existing blocks.  If the final position is \(i\) from the left, the new
inversions involving \(r\) are exactly the minima of the blocks to its right, so
their number is \(k-i\).  Summing over \(i=1,\ldots,k\) contributes
\([k]_q\) in each of the two cases, giving the recurrence above and the initial
condition.

Now restrict this insertion argument to insertions of \(r+1\) into an existing
block of an ordered partition \(\tau\in\OP_{r,k}\).  The result is precisely an
ordered partition \(\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\) in which \(r+1\) is not a singleton,
and the extra inversion contribution is again one of \(0,1,\ldots,k-1\).
Hence
\[
 [k]_qF_{r,k}
 =
 \sum_{\substack{\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\\ r+1\text{ not singleton}}}
      q^{\inv(\sigma)} .
\]
Substituting this into (3), and replacing \(r+1\) by \(r\), gives exactly
\[
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b
=
\sum_{\substack{2\le r\le n\\1\le k\le r-1}}
\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in \OP_{r,k}\\r\text{ not singleton}}}
q^{\inv(\sigma)}z^{r-k-1}.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^{a-1}z^b\) proves the claimed interpretation.
\end{proof}

\bibitem{Lentfer}

## Referee report on the revised `answer.tex`

### Overall verdict

The revised draft is **answer-ready**. I re-read the proof in full and found no mathematical gap affecting validity. The revision addresses the earlier minor concerns: it now states the \(q\)-Stirling boundary conditions, clarifies the super-Schur notation \(s_\lambda(q/z)\), explains how the Frobenius/character-level supersymmetry result gives the Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\), and tightens the theorem statement to \(2\le r\le n\).

### Literature validation

The interpretation of \(R_n^{(m)}\) as a diagonal coinvariant algebra is consistent with Bergeron’s definition as a quotient by the ideal generated by constant-term-free diagonal invariants, and Bergeron’s result supports a universal Schur-positive Hilbert expansion independent of the number of variable sets. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S000187081300056X?utm_source=openai))

The use of diagonal supersymmetry is supported by Lentfer’s theorem: bosonic–fermionic coinvariant rings have character/Hilbert expansions in super Schur functions with universal coefficients independent of the numbers of even and odd alphabets. Lentfer’s Proposition 5.1 gives the Hilbert-series version used here, and Lemma 4.4 gives exactly the one-even/one-odd specialization \(s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\), with non-hooks vanishing. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885)) ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885)) ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The Rhoades–Wilson formula used in the proof is also correctly quoted: their article states
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n z^{n-k}[k]!_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)
\]
with the same \(q\)-Stirling recurrence and initial condition. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

### LaTeX contract audit

I recreated and compiled the revised `answer.tex` with `pdflatex` twice.

- Document class is exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`.
- `fullpage` is used and no forbidden margin/layout packages or manual layout changes appear.
- No forbidden line-spacing commands appear.
- No in-document font-size commands such as `\small`, `\footnotesize`, `\scriptsize`, or `\fontsize` appear.
- The file is standalone; `references.bib` is unused but irrelevant.
- Compilation succeeds.
- The generated PDF is 3 pages, within the 12-page limit.

No LaTeX-contract violation was found.

### Paragraph-by-paragraph mathematical audit

1. **Problem statement and interpretation.**  
   The interpretation as the diagonal coinvariant algebra is explicitly recorded. This satisfies the ambiguity requirement in the prompt. The statement that \(c_\lambda(n)\) are stable \(GL_m\)-multiplicities is correct.

2. **Definition of ordered set partitions and inversions.**  
   The inversion statistic is well-defined. For singleton blocks it reduces to the ordinary inversion statistic on permutations read from left to right. No issue.

3. **Theorem statement.**  
   The revised range \(2\le r\le n\) is cleaner and correct. The condition \(r-k=b+1\) forces \(k\le r-1\), and the “\(r\) not singleton” condition is unambiguous. Edge case \(n=1\) is handled: the sum is empty and only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).

4. **Supersymmetry reduction.**  
   The revised explanation now explicitly says that after applying the dimension map to the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-Frobenius factor, one obtains the Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\). This resolves the previous possible ambiguity. The proof is relying on a cited external theorem, but the cited theorem supplies exactly the needed input.

5. **Super Schur specialization.**  
   The formula
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/z)=q^az^b+q^{a-1}z^{b+1}
   \]
   is correct for one even and one odd variable. Non-hooks vanish because the \((1,1)\)-hook condition is \(\lambda_2\le 1\). The resulting identity
   \[
   H_n(q,z)=1+(q+z)\sum_{a,b}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b
   \]
   is valid.

6. **Rhoades–Wilson formula and recurrence manipulation.**  
   The boundary conditions have been added and are correct. The algebraic derivation of
   \[
   \frac{H_n(q,z)-1}{q+z}
   =
   \sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\sum_{k=1}^{r}[k]_qF_{r,k}z^{r-k}
   \]
   is correct. I checked the indexing carefully; there is no off-by-one error.

7. **Identification of \(F_{r,k}\) with ordered-set-partition inversion enumerators.**  
   The recurrence proof is correct. A minor optional wording improvement would be to say explicitly that the singleton-insertion case starts from \(\OP_{r-1,k-1}\), while the existing-block case starts from \(\OP_{r-1,k}\). However, this is clear from the displayed recurrence and does not constitute a gap.

8. **Restricted insertion argument.**  
   The bijection is correct: inserting \(r+1\) into an existing block of \(\tau\in\OP_{r,k}\) gives exactly a partition of \([r+1]\) into \(k\) blocks in which \(r+1\) is not singleton. The additional inversion count is \(0,1,\ldots,k-1\), producing the factor \([k]_q\).

9. **Final reindexing and coefficient extraction.**  
   Replacing \(r+1\) by \(r\) correctly changes the exponent \(z^{r-k}\) into \(z^{r-k-1}\), and the extraction of the coefficient of \(q^{a-1}z^b\) gives precisely the stated combinatorial interpretation.

10. **Empty partition coefficient.**  
   \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\) follows from the constant term of the Hilbert series. No other hook term contributes degree zero, so there is no ambiguity.

### Computational cross-check

I independently enumerated ordered set partitions and the stated inversion statistic, and compared the resulting generating functions with the recurrence form of \((H_n(q,z)-1)/(q+z)\) for \(1\le n\le 6\). The two sides agreed in all tested cases. This also confirms the corrected sanity-check convention in the revised notes.

### Remaining issues

No mathematical or LaTeX-contract issue remains. The only possible refinements are stylistic: cite Lentfer’s Proposition 5.1/Lemma 4.4 more precisely in the prose, and make the two starting block counts in the insertion proof completely explicit. Neither is needed for correctness.
